topics.title: Familiarisation of Arduino IDE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCADL507 - ROBOTICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Familiarisation of Arduino IDE # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Arduino IDE (Integrated Development Environment)** is a cross-platform, open-source software application written in Java that provides a unified workspace for writing, compiling (verifying), debugging, and uploading program code (called **Sketches**) to Arduino-compatible microcontroller boards. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCADL507 (Robotics Lab)**, familiarisation of the Arduino IDE constitutes a foundational laboratory competency. The IDE abstracts low-level hardware programming by offering a simplified C/C++-based framework built upon the **Wiring** and **Processing** platforms. The IDE performs three critical functions in a robotics workflow: 1. **Code Authoring** — Provides a text editor with syntax highlighting, auto-indentation, and bracket matching. 2. **Compilation** — Translates human-readable Arduino code into machine-readable **HEX** files using the `avr-gcc` toolchain (or `arm-none-eabi-gcc` for ARM-based boards). 3. **Upload/Debugging** — Transmits the compiled HEX file to the microcontroller's flash memory via a serial bootloader over **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** protocol. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Terminology for KTU Viva:** > - **Sketch**: The source code file (.ino) written by the user. > - **Board Manager**: Plugin system for adding support to various microcontroller boards. > - **Library Manager**: Repository for reusable code modules (e.g., Servo.h, Wire.h). > - **Serial Monitor**: Debugging tool for bi-directional communication with the board at standard baud rates (typically **9600 bps**). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Workshop Bench Imagine you are a **mechanic building a custom robot vehicle**. Before you can assemble anything, you need a **workbench** that holds all your tools in one organized place — screwdrivers, spanners, soldering iron, instruction manuals, and a testing station. The **Arduino IDE is exactly this workbench for a robotics programmer**: | Workshop Tool | Arduino IDE Equivalent | |---------------|------------------------| | Workbench surface | Code Editor window | | Instruction manual | Built-in Examples & Reference | | Soldering/Assembly tools | Compile & Upload buttons | | Test diagnostic machine | Serial Monitor / Serial Plotter | | Spare parts catalogue | Library Manager | | Different vehicle models | Board Manager (Uno, Mega, Nano, ESP32) | Just as a mechanic can swap tools depending on the car being repaired, the IDE allows the user to **switch the target board and port** so the same code can be deployed across different microcontrollers without changing the development environment. --- ## 1.3 System Requirements & Standard Metrics The Arduino IDE officially supports the following operating systems with the stated minimum requirements: - **Windows**: Windows 7 or higher, ~200 MB disk space - **macOS**: macOS 10.10 (Yosemite) or higher - **Linux**: Ubuntu 14.04+ or equivalent (via `.tar.xz` archive or `apt`) > [!TIP] > The current stable IDE is **Arduino IDE 2.x**, which is built upon the **Eclipse Theia** framework and supports **intelligent autocompletion**, **real-time error reporting**, and a **modern debugger** — a significant upgrade from the legacy 1.8.x release. **Standard Communication Baud Rates** supported by the Serial Monitor: $$9600, \ 19200, \ 38400, \ 57600, \ 115200 \text{ bps}$$ The default Arduino bootloader operates at **57600 bps** for the Uno R3, while the Serial Monitor default is typically **9600 bps** — a common mismatch that students must reconcile in their `setup()` function using `Serial.begin(baud_rate)`. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Breakdown of the Arduino IDE The Arduino IDE is built upon a layered architecture. Understanding this structure is essential for KTU viva questions on "Explain the components of the Arduino IDE." ### Layer 1 — User Interface Layer (Front-End) - **Editor Pane**: Where the `.ino` sketch is authored. - **Message Pane** (Black console at the bottom): Displays compilation errors, warnings, and HEX file size. - **Toolbar**: Contains `Verify (✓)`, `Upload (→)`, `New`, `Open`, `Save`, `Serial Monitor`. ### Layer 2 — Preprocessing & Compilation Layer (Middle-End) - **Arduino Preprocessor**: Injects hidden `main.cpp` containing `setup()` and `loop()` glue code, and includes library headers. - **`avr-gcc` Compiler**: Converts C/C++ to assembly language (target: **AVR architecture** for Uno/Nano; **ARM Cortex** for Due/Zero). - **`avr-objcopy`**: Converts ELF output to Intel HEX format. - **`avrdude`**: The AVRDUDE utility that flashes HEX to the microcontroller over serial. ### Layer 3 — Hardware Abstraction Layer (Back-End) - **Bootloader**: A small pre-burned program in the MCU that listens for new code over UART. - **GPIO Pin Mapping**: Each Arduino digital pin (0–13 on Uno) corresponds to a specific AVR port register bit (e.g., Pin 13 → PB5 on ATmega328P). ## 2.2 The Sketch Program Structure (Mandatory for KTU) Every Arduino sketch follows a **strict two-function template** that is auto-injected by the preprocessor: ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────┐ │ // 1. HEADER COMMENTS │ ← Optional metadata │ // 2. #include directives │ ← Library imports │ // 3. #define macros │ ← Symbolic constants │ // 4. Global variable decl. │ ├─────────────────────────────────┤ │ void setup() { } │ ← Runs ONCE on power-up/reset ├─────────────────────────────────┤ │ void loop() { } │ ← Runs INFINITELY in a cycle └─────────────────────────────────┘ ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The `setup()` and `loop()` functions are **non-negotiable** in standard Arduino programming. If a student omits them, the compiler throws the error: *"error: 'setup' was not declared in this scope"*. ## 2.3 KTU Reference Cheat Sheet — IDE Menu Functions | Menu Item | Function | KTU Viva Importance | |-----------|----------|---------------------| | **File → New** | Creates a blank sketch | ⭐ Basic | | **File → Examples** | Built-in tutorials (Blink, Fade, etc.) | ⭐⭐⭐ High | | **Sketch → Verify/Compile** | Converts code to HEX; checks syntax | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Sketch → Upload** | Flashes HEX to MCU via bootloader | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Sketch → Include Library** | Adds `#include ` line | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Tools → Board** | Selects target microcontroller | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Tools → Port** | Selects COM/tty port (e.g., `/dev/ttyUSB0`) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Tools → Serial Monitor** | Opens debugging terminal at 9600 baud | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Tools → Serial Plotter** | Real-time graphical data plotter | ⭐⭐ | | **Tools → Board Manager** | Adds support for new boards (ESP, STM) | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Tools → Library Manager** | Downloads third-party libraries | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Help → Reference** | Offline documentation for all functions | ⭐⭐ | ## 2.4 The Compilation-to-Upload Pipeline (Engineering Utility) The IDE's workflow represents a standard **embedded systems build pipeline** found in industry-grade toolchains like **STM32CubeIDE**, **ESP-IDF**, and **MPLAB X**. Understanding this pipeline equips the student to transition from hobbyist Arduino programming to **professional firmware development** in their higher semesters and careers. | Stage | Tool Used | Output | |-------|-----------|--------| | Preprocessing | `arduino-builder` | Expanded `.cpp` with hidden `main()` | | Compilation | `avr-gcc` | Object files (`.o`) | | Linking | `avr-gcc` | ELF executable | | Object Copy | `avr-objcopy` | Intel HEX (`.hex`) | | Upload | `avrdude` | Flash memory write via UART | | Verification | `avrdude -v` | Read-back signature check | ## 2.5 Common Error Categories & Debugging Logic > [!TIP] > **KTU students frequently lose marks** by failing to recognize the difference between **compilation errors** (code-side) and **upload errors** (driver/port-side). | Error Type | Symptom | Fix | |------------|---------|-----| | Syntax Error | Red underline in editor | Missing `;`, `}`, or `()` | | Board Mismatch | "Programmer not responding" | Select correct board in **Tools → Board** | | Port Busy | "Serial port 'COM3' already in use" | Close Serial Monitor; close other IDEs | | Driver Missing | Port not listed | Install **CH340** or **CP2102** USB driver | | Sync Error | "avrdude: stk500_recv(): programmer is not responding" | Press reset button; check TX/RX crossover | # 3. Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Workflow ## 3.1 Exhaustive Lab Procedure — Writing Your First Sketch The following is the **standard KTU laboratory procedure** for verifying that the Arduino IDE is correctly installed and communicating with the board. The exercise is universally known as the **"Blink" program** and serves as the canonical "Hello, World!" equivalent for embedded systems. ### Step 1 — Launch the Arduino IDE Double-click the Arduino icon. The IDE opens with a default blank sketch named `sketch_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS`. The cursor is positioned inside `setup()`. ### Step 2 — Configure the Target Board Navigate to: **Tools → Board → Arduino AVR Boards → Arduino Uno** This selection informs the compiler which pin mappings, clock frequency (16 MHz for Uno), and bootloader protocol to use. ### Step 3 — Select the Serial Port Navigate to: **Tools → Port → COM3 (or /dev/ttyUSB0 on Linux, /dev/cu.usbmodem on macOS)** > [!WARNING] > If no port is visible, the **USB driver** is missing. On Windows, install the **CH340/CH341** driver (for clone boards) or the **ATmega16U2** driver (for official boards). ### Step 4 — Author the Sketch Type or paste the following code into the editor: ```cpp /* KTU PCADL507 - Experiment 2 Program: LED Blink (Arduino IDE Familiarisation) Board: Arduino Uno Author: Date: */ // Pin 13 is connected to the built-in LED on most Arduino boards int ledPin = 13; void setup() { // Configure the LED pin as OUTPUT pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT); // Initialise serial communication at 9600 bps for debugging Serial.begin(9600); Serial.println("KTU Robotics Lab - Setup Initialised"); } void loop() { digitalWrite(ledPin, HIGH); // Turn LED ON (5V at pin) Serial.println("LED State: HIGH"); delay(1000); // Wait for 1000 ms = 1 second digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW); // Turn LED OFF (0V at pin) Serial.println("LED State: LOW"); delay(1000); // Wait for 1 second } ``` ### Step 5 — Verify (Compile) Click the **✓ Verify** button (or press `Ctrl+R` / `Cmd+R`). The IDE performs the following sequence: ``` [1] Preprocessing → adds hidden main(), includes Arduino.h [2] Compilation → avr-gcc produces object code [3] Linking → combines objects into single ELF [4] HEX generation → avr-objcopy produces .hex file [5] Size report → "Binary sketch size: 1,924 bytes (of 32,256 maximum)" ``` A successful compile displays the message **"Done compiling"** in the message pane in **green text**. A failed compile displays **"Error compiling"** in **orange/red text**. ### Step 6 — Upload to the Board Click the **→ Upload** button (or press `Ctrl+U` / `Cmd+U`). The IDE executes `avrdude` which: 1. Resets the microcontroller via the **DTR (Data Terminal Ready)** line of the USB-UART bridge. 2. The bootloader detects the reset and enters **programming mode**. 3. The HEX file is transmitted in **Intel HEX record format** over UART at **115200 bps**. 4. The bootloader writes each record into flash memory using **SPM (Store Program Memory)** instructions. A successful upload displays: **"Done uploading"** and the on-board LED labeled **"L"** begins blinking at 1 Hz. ### Step 7 — Open the Serial Monitor Navigate to **Tools → Serial Monitor** (or press `Ctrl+Shift+M`). Ensure the baud rate dropdown is set to **9600**. The output should read: ``` KTU Robotics Lab - Setup Initialised LED State: HIGH LED State: LOW LED State: HIGH LED State: LOW ... ``` ### Step 8 — Verify Physical LED Blinking Observe the on-board LED (Pin 13). It must toggle **ON for 1 second** and **OFF for 1 second** in a continuous cycle. This confirms end-to-end functionality of the IDE. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Time Analysis of the IDE Workflow | Operation | Typical Duration | Bottleneck Source | |-----------|------------------|-------------------| | IDE cold start | 3–8 seconds | JVM initialization | | Verify (compile) | 2–5 seconds | `avr-gcc` invocation | | Upload to board | 5–10 seconds | UART transmission at 115200 bps | | Serial Monitor open | <1 second | Local socket | | Library download (Library Manager) | 5–30 seconds | Internet bandwidth | > [!NOTE] > The total time to **Verify + Upload** is approximately **7–15 seconds** for a simple sketch. This serves as a benchmark; if upload takes significantly longer, the COM port may be conflicting with another application. ## 3.3 Mathematical Foundation — UART Timing For KTU students who must relate baud rate to actual transmission time, the following derivation applies: $$\text{Time per bit} = \frac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$$ For a 115200 bps upload: $$T_{bit} = \frac{1}{115200} \approx 8.68 \ \mu s \text{ per bit}$$ Each HEX record uses **10 bits per byte** (8 data + 1 start + 1 stop bit). Therefore: $$T_{byte} = 10 \times 8.68 \ \mu s = 86.8 \ \mu s$$ For a sketch of size $N$ bytes (e.g., 1924 bytes for Blink): $$T_{upload} = N \times T_{byte} = 1924 \times 86.8 \ \mu s \approx 0.167 \text{ seconds}$$ The observed **5–10 second upload time** is dominated by **bootloader handshaking, flash page erases, and write cycles**, not raw UART throughput. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Arduino IDE — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB UserInput["User Typed Code .ino File"] --> EditorPane["Editor Pane
Syntax Highlighting"] EditorPane --> ClickVerify["Click Verify Button"] EditorPane --> ClickUpload["Click Upload Button"] ClickVerify --> Preprocessor["Arduino Preprocessor
Hidden main.cpp injection"] Preprocessor --> Compiler["avr-gcc Compiler
C to AVR Assembly"] Compiler --> Linker["Linker
Combines object files"] Linker --> ObjCopy["avr-objcopy
ELF to Intel HEX"] ObjCopy --> HexFile["HEX File Ready"] HexFile --> MsgPane["Message Pane
Size Report"] ClickUpload --> Avrdude["avrdude Utility"] Avrdude --> UartBridge["USB to UART Bridge
CH340 or ATmega16U2"] UartBridge --> Bootloader["AVR Bootloader
Listens on DTR Reset"] Bootloader --> FlashMem["Flash Memory
32 KB on Uno"] FlashMem --> McuRun["Microcontroller Executes Code"] ToolsMenu["Tools Menu Access"] --> SerialMon["Serial Monitor
9600 bps Debug Terminal"] ToolsMenu --> LibraryMgr["Library Manager
Download Third Party"] ToolsMenu --> BoardMgr["Board Manager
Install Board Packages"] ``` ## 4.2 IDE Workflow — Sequential State Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle: IDE Launched Idle --> Authoring: User Types Code Authoring --> Verifying: Click Verify Verifying --> CompileError: Syntax Issue Found CompileError --> Authoring: Fix Errors Verifying --> Compiled: Done Compiling Green Message Compiled --> Uploading: Click Upload Uploading --> UploadError: Port or Driver Issue UploadError --> ConfigPort: Select Correct Port ConfigPort --> Uploading: Retry Upload Uploading --> Running: Done Uploading LED Blinks Running --> Debugging: Open Serial Monitor Debugging --> Authoring: Modify Code and Repeat Running --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Pin-to-Register Mapping (ATmega328P — Arduino Uno) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Arduino Digital Pin 13"] --> B["AVR Port B Bit 5
PB5"] B --> C["ATmega328P
Physical Pin 19"] C --> D["On Board LED L"] E["Arduino Analog Pin A0"] --> F["AVR Port C Bit 0
PC0"] F --> G["ADC Channel 0
10 bit Resolution"] G --> H["Sensor Input"] I["Arduino Pin 0 RX"] --> J["PD0 RXD"] K["Arduino Pin 1 TX"] --> L["PD1 TXD"] ``` ## 4.4 Compilation Pipeline (Detailed View) ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["Step 1 sketch.ino"] --> S2["Step 2 Preprocessed cpp
Includes hidden main"] S2 --> S3["Step 3 Assembly Output
avr-gcc -S"] S3 --> S4["Step 4 Object Files
avr-gcc -c"] S4 --> S5["Step 5 ELF Executable
avr-gcc linker"] S5 --> S6["Step 6 Intel HEX File
avr-objcopy"] S6 --> S7["Step 7 Upload via avrdude"] S7 --> S8["Step 8 Flash Memory"] S8 --> S9["Step 9 Microcontroller Runs
setup once loop forever"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Diagram Interpretation Note:** In your lab record, you may be asked to draw the IDE architecture or the upload pipeline. The above Mermaid blocks are visual study aids; redraw them neatly in your **lab record book** using boxes and arrows. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] **Define Arduino IDE. List any four important features of it.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Arduino IDE is an **open-source, cross-platform Integrated Development Environment** used to write, compile, and upload code to Arduino microcontroller boards. It is written in Java and based on the Processing platform. Four important features: 1. **Cross-platform compatibility** — Runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux. 2. **Built-in library manager** — Simplifies inclusion of external libraries. 3. **Integrated Serial Monitor** — Enables real-time debugging via UART. 4. **One-click Verify and Upload** — Abstracts the complex toolchain (avr-gcc + avrdude) for beginners. *(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each for features = 3 marks)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **Differentiate between `setup()` and `loop()` functions in an Arduino sketch.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | `setup()` | `loop()` | |--------|-----------|----------| | Execution | Runs **once** after every reset/power-up | Runs **infinitely** in a continuous cycle | | Purpose | Initialise pins, serial, peripherals | Contain the main program logic | | Mandatory | Yes, if omitted — compilation error | Yes, if omitted — compilation error | | Typical Use | `pinMode()`, `Serial.begin()` | `digitalWrite()`, `sensorRead()` | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Answer any **ONE** full question from the module. Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample] **Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and major components of the Arduino IDE**. Mention the role of `avr-gcc` and `avrdude` in the build pipeline. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an Arduino program to **blink the on-board LED connected to pin 13** with a 500 ms ON and 500 ms OFF interval. Also display the LED state on the Serial Monitor. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — IDE Architecture (7 Marks) The Arduino IDE consists of three major functional layers: **1. Editor Layer (User Interface)** - The text editor where the user types the `.ino` sketch. - Provides **syntax highlighting**, **auto-indent**, and **bracket matching**. - Includes a message pane (bottom console) for compiler feedback. **2. Compiler Layer (Toolchain)** - `avr-gcc` — The GNU C/C++ compiler for AVR architecture. It converts the preprocessed C++ code into AVR assembly and produces an **ELF (Executable and Linkable Format)** file. The target architecture is **AVR 8-bit RISC** for Uno/Nano. - `avr-objcopy` — Translates ELF into **Intel HEX format** (.hex) suitable for the bootloader. **3. Upload Layer (Deployment)** - `avrdude` — The AVR Downloader/UploaDEr utility. It transmits the HEX file to the microcontroller's flash memory using the **STK500** protocol over UART through a USB-to-serial bridge (CH340 or ATmega16U2). ``` [Sketch .ino] → [Preprocessor] → [avr-gcc] → [ELF] → [avr-objcopy] → [.hex] → [avrdude] → [Flash Memory] ``` *(1 mark for block diagram + 3 marks for layered explanation + 1 mark each for avr-gcc and avrdude roles = 7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Blink Program (7 Marks) ```cpp /* KTU PCADL507 - Experiment 2 Program: Blink LED with Serial Monitor Output */ int ledPin = 13; // Built-in LED on Arduino Uno void setup() { pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT); // Configure Pin 13 as output Serial.begin(9600); // Initialise UART at 9600 bps Serial.println("System Initialised"); } void loop() { digitalWrite(ledPin, HIGH); // LED ON Serial.println("LED: ON"); delay(500); // Wait 500 ms digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW); // LED OFF Serial.println("LED: OFF"); delay(500); // Wait 500 ms } ``` **Explanation of key lines:** - `pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT)` — Sets pin 13 as output direction in the DDR (Data Direction Register). - `Serial.begin(9600)` — Configures UART registers UBRR, UCSR for 9600 bps at 16 MHz clock. - `digitalWrite(HIGH)` — Drives pin to **5V logic level**. - `delay(500)` — Uses Timer0 to pause execution for 500 milliseconds. *(1 mark program header + 2 marks for setup + 3 marks for loop logic + 1 mark explanation = 7 marks)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Alternate Choice] **Q. (a)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure to install Arduino IDE** on a Windows PC and configure it for an Arduino Uno board. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the various **menus and toolbar buttons** of the Arduino IDE with a screenshot-based description. Discuss the purpose of the **Serial Monitor** and **Serial Plotter** tools. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Installation Procedure (7 Marks) **Step 1:** Visit the official website `https://www.arduino.cc/en/software` and download the **Windows Installer (.exe)** for Arduino IDE 2.x. **Step 2:** Run the installer with administrator privileges. Accept the license agreement and click **Next**. **Step 3:** Select the installation directory (default: `C:\Program Files\Arduino IDE`) and click **Install**. **Step 4:** During installation, allow the **USB driver** installation prompt. This installs the **CH340/CH341** driver for clone boards and the **ATmega16U2** driver for official boards. **Step 5:** Launch the IDE. Navigate to **Tools → Board → Boards Manager**, search for "Arduino AVR Boards", and click **Install**. **Step 6:** Connect the Arduino Uno via USB cable. Open **Device Manager** and identify the **COM port** (e.g., COM3). **Step 7:** In the IDE, navigate to **Tools → Port → COM3** to select the detected port. **Step 8:** Upload the **Blink example** from **File → Examples → 01.Basics → Blink** to verify successful installation. *(½ mark per step, 8 steps = 4 marks + 3 marks for driver explanation and verification = 7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — IDE Menus & Tools (7 Marks) **1. File Menu** — New, Open, Save, Examples (built-in tutorials like Blink, Fade, ReadAnalogVoltage). **2. Edit Menu** — Cut, Copy, Paste, Find, Comment/Uncomment (Ctrl+/). **3. Sketch Menu** — Verify/Compile (Ctrl+R), Upload (Ctrl+U), Include Library. **4. Tools Menu** — Board selection, Port selection, Serial Monitor, Serial Plotter. **5. Help Menu** — Reference for built-in functions, Get Help, About. **Toolbar Buttons:** - **✓ Verify** — Compiles the code without uploading. - **→ Upload** — Compiles and flashes the HEX to the board. - **🆕 New, 📂 Open, 💾 Save** — File operations. **Serial Monitor** (`Ctrl+Shift+M`): A **text-based debugging terminal** that exchanges data with the Arduino over UART. It operates at user-selectable baud rates. Used to display sensor readings, debug messages, and accept user input sent back to the board. **Serial Plotter** (`Ctrl+Shift+L`): A **real-time graphical visualisation tool** that automatically plots numeric values printed via `Serial.println()`. Ideal for visualising sensor waveforms (e.g., temperature over time, accelerometer data). *(3 marks menus + 2 marks toolbar + 2 marks Serial Monitor/Plotter = 7 marks)* --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse** `Verify` with `Upload`. Verify only compiles; Upload compiles AND flashes. *(−1 mark penalty if confused in viva)* > 2. **Always match** the baud rate in `Serial.begin()` with the Serial Monitor dropdown — otherwise you will see **garbled characters**. *(−2 marks if serial output question is answered incorrectly)* > 3. **Mention the full form** of `avrdude` (AVR Downloader/UploaDEr) — KTU examiners explicitly test this acronym. *(−1 mark if omitted)* > 4. **Distinguish** between the **Bootloader** (resides in MCU) and the **Compiler** (runs on PC). Students often conflate the two. > 5. In code, **always include `pinMode()` in `setup()`**, not `loop()`. Calling pinMode in loop wastes CPU cycles. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - ✅ **Arduino IDE = Editor + Compiler + Uploader** — three roles in one tool. - ✅ **Sketch file extension**: `.ino` (e.g., `Blink.ino`) - ✅ **Two mandatory functions**: `setup()` runs **once**; `loop()` runs **forever**. - ✅ **Build Pipeline**: `.ino` → preprocess → `avr-gcc` → `.elf` → `avr-objcopy` → `.hex` → `avrdude` → Flash. - ✅ **Default baud rate** of Arduino Serial Monitor = **9600 bps**. - ✅ **Bootloader** is a small program pre-flashed in the MCU that listens for new code via UART. - ✅ **USB-to-Serial Bridge** chips: **CH340/CH341** (clones) and **ATmega16U2** (official Uno). - ✅ **Verify (✓)** = compile only; **Upload (→)** = compile + flash to board. - ✅ **Arduino Uno** uses the **ATmega328P** microcontroller running at **16 MHz** clock. - ✅ **Pin 13** has a **built-in LED** — perfect for first test program. - ✅ **Library Manager** is used to install external libraries (e.g., Servo, Wire, SPI, LiquidCrystal). - ✅ **Board Manager** is used to add support for non-AVR boards (ESP32, STM32, Teensy). - ✅ **Serial.println()** prints data with a newline; **Serial.print()** prints without a newline. - ✅ **delay(ms)** pauses execution — maximum reliable delay ≈ 49.7 days (32-bit unsigned). - ✅ For KTU lab record: **always include the circuit diagram, code, observation, and result**. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip:** When asked *"Explain Arduino IDE"*, always structure your answer in **3 layers** (Editor / Compiler / Uploader), mention **2 acronyms** (`avr-gcc`, `avrdude`), and describe **at least 1 menu** (Tools or Sketch). This guarantees a **full-mark answer** under KTU's structured valuation scheme.

topics.title: Arduino Microcontroller I/O Interfacing (LED, Serial Monitor)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADL507 - ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Arduino Microcontroller I/O Interfacing (LED, Serial Monitor)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: ARDUINO MICROCONTROLLER I/O INTERFACING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Arduino** is an open-source electronics prototyping platform based on flexible, easy-to-use **hardware (ATmega328P microcontroller-based boards)** and **software (Arduino IDE)**. It is intended for artists, designers, hobbyists, and anyone interested in creating interactive objects or environments.

**I/O Interfacing** in the context of Arduino refers to the controlled exchange of **digital/analog signals** between the microcontroller and external peripherals (such as LEDs, sensors, actuators) through its **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** pins.

**Serial Monitor** is a built-in debugging tool in the Arduino IDE that communicates with the Arduino board over a **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** interface, allowing real-time two-way textual data exchange between the PC and the microcontroller.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Arduino as a Universal Translator/Postman"**
> Imagine the Arduino board as a **postman** standing between two worlds:
> - **Your Computer (The Boss)** — gives instructions in code.
> - **The Physical World (LEDs, motors, sensors)** — only understands voltage and current.
>
> The **GPIO pins** are the postman's **hands** that deliver letters (5V = ON, 0V = OFF) and bring back mail (sensor readings). The **Serial Monitor** is the **walkie-talkie** the postman uses to confirm: *"Sir, I delivered the letter to LED pin 13. It's glowing now."*

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Arduino Logic HIGH | **5 V** (Uno) / **3.3 V** (Due) | Volts |
| Arduino Logic LOW | **0 V** (GND) | Volts |
| DC Current per I/O Pin | **40 mA** (Absolute Max) | Milliampere |
| Recommended Pin Current | **20 mA** | Milliampere |
| Standard Red LED Forward Voltage ($V_f$) | **2.0 V** | Volts |
| ATmega328P Clock Frequency | **16 MHz** | Megahertz |
| Default Serial Baud Rate | **9600 bps** | Bits per second |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Arduino I/O Interfacing

#### Pillar 1: The pinMode() Configuration

Every GPIO pin on the ATmega328P can operate in one of three modes:

1. **INPUT** — Pin reads external voltage (0V to 5V). High-impedance state (~100 MΩ).
2. **OUTPUT** — Pin sources/sinks current to drive loads like LEDs.
3. **INPUT_PULLUP** — Internal 20 kΩ–50 kΩ resistor activated (saves external components).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Configure?** An unconfigured pin is in a "high-impedance" floating state — sensitive to electrical noise, like a feather that flutters with every breath of wind. `pinMode()` anchors it firmly.

#### Pillar 2: The digitalWrite() / digitalRead() Logic

| Function | Direction | Logic HIGH (5V) | Logic LOW (0V) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH)` | OUTPUT | LED ON (current flows) | — |
| `digitalWrite(pin, LOW)` | OUTPUT | — | LED OFF (no current) |
| `digitalRead(pin)` | INPUT | Returns `1` | Returns `0` |

#### Pillar 3: The Serial Monitor Communication (UART)

Serial communication is **asynchronous**, meaning no shared clock line exists. Both ends must **agree on timing (baud rate)** beforehand.

- **Baud Rate**: Number of signal changes per second. 9600 baud = 9600 bits/sec.
- **Frame Structure**: 1 Start bit + 8 Data bits + (Optional Parity) + 1 Stop bit = **10 bits/frame**.

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial robotics, the same UART protocol is used to communicate with **motor drivers (L298N)**, **IMU sensors (MPU6050)**, and **wireless modules (HC-05 Bluetooth)**. The Serial Monitor is the engineer's **stethoscope** — used for debugging sensor fusion algorithms, monitoring joint angles, and verifying actuator response in real-time.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Avoid the `|` character inside table rows. Use `\vert` instead.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ohm's Law** | $V = I \cdot R$ | Basis for resistor selection |
| 2 | **Current Limiting Resistor (LED)** | $R = \dfrac{V_{supply} - V_{f}}{I_{LED}}$ | Protects LED from burning out |
| 3 | **Power Dissipated in Resistor** | $P_R = I^{2} \cdot R$ | Verify resistor wattage rating |
| 4 | **Baud Rate to Bit Period** | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | Timing analysis of UART frames |
| 5 | **Byte Transmission Time** | $T_{byte} = \dfrac{10}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | 8N1 = 10 bits per byte |
| 6 | **ATmega328P Bit Toggle Rate** | $f_{toggle} = \dfrac{16\,\text{MHz}}{2N}$ | N = prescaler divisor |
| 7 | **Voltage Divider (Sensor Reading)** | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Interfacing 5V sensor to 3.3V MCU |
| 8 | **PWM Duty Cycle** | $D\% = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100$ | LED brightness / motor speed |
| 9 | **ADC Resolution (10-bit)** | $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1024}$ | Analog sensor reading |
| 10 | **Pull-up Equivalent Resistance** | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_{internal} \cdot R_{external}}{R_{internal} + R_{external}}$ | INPUT_PULLUP design |

### 2.4 LED Interfacing — The "Why" Behind the Resistor

An LED is a **non-ohmic device**. Its resistance drops dramatically once the **forward voltage ($V_f$)** is exceeded, leading to a **runaway current increase** that destroys the junction thermally within milliseconds. The **current limiting resistor** acts as a **sacrificial guardian**, absorbing the excess voltage and clamping the current to a safe value.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Current Limiting Resistor Value for a 5mm Red LED

**Given Problem (Standard KTU Numerical):**
- Supply Voltage: $V_{s} = 5\,V$ (Arduino digital pin HIGH)
- LED Forward Voltage Drop: $V_{f} = 2.0\,V$ (standard red LED)
- Desired LED Current: $I_{LED} = 20\,mA = 0.020\,A$
- Find: Required series resistance $R$

---

**Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the LED-Resistor loop.**

The supply voltage must equal the sum of voltage drops across the resistor and the LED.

$$
V_s = V_R + V_f
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to isolate the voltage drop across the resistor.**

$$
V_R = V_s - V_f
$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
V_R = 5\,V - 2.0\,V = 3.0\,V
$$

*Logic: The resistor must "absorb" the 3.0 V difference between the Arduino's 5V output and the LED's 2V intrinsic drop.*

**Step 4: Apply Ohm's Law to the resistor.**

By definition, resistance equals voltage divided by current.

$$
R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_{LED}}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute numerical values.**

$$
R = \dfrac{3.0\,V}{0.020\,A}
$$

**Step 6: Perform the division.**

$$
R = 150\,\Omega
$$

**Step 7: Apply Engineering Safety Margin.**

Since 150 Ω is a non-standard value, we select the **next higher standard E12 series** resistor to ensure LED current does not exceed 20 mA.

$$
R_{standard} = 220\,\Omega
$$

**Step 8: Verify actual current with the chosen resistor.**

$$
I_{actual} = \dfrac{V_s - V_f}{R_{standard}} = \dfrac{3.0\,V}{220\,\Omega}
$$

$$
I_{actual} = 0.01364\,A \approx 13.64\,mA
$$

*Conclusion: 13.64 mA is well within the safe 20 mA limit and produces a bright visible glow.*

**Step 9: Calculate Resistor Power Rating (for safety).**

$$
P_R = I_{actual}^{2} \times R_{standard}
$$

$$
P_R = (0.01364)^{2} \times 220 = 0.0409\,W
$$

A standard **¼ Watt (0.25 W) resistor** is more than sufficient (safety factor ≈ 6×).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Baud Rate Timing Derivation

| Step | Parameter | Derivation Logic | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Given Baud Rate | Standard Arduino default | $B = 9600\,\text{bps}$ |
| 2 | Time per Bit | Inverse of baud rate: $T_{bit} = 1/B$ | $T_{bit} = 104.17\,\mu s$ |
| 3 | Bits per Frame (8N1) | 1 Start + 8 Data + 1 Stop = 10 | $N_{bits} = 10$ |
| 4 | Time per Byte | $T_{byte} = N_{bits} \times T_{bit}$ | $T_{byte} = 1.0417\,ms$ |
| 5 | Bytes per Second | $R_{bytes} = 9600 / 10$ | $R_{bytes} = 960\,\text{bytes/s}$ |
| 6 | 16 MHz Clock Ticks per Bit | $N_{ticks} = 16\,\text{MHz} \times T_{bit}$ | $N_{ticks} = 1667\,\text{cycles}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The ATmega328P's UART hardware divides its 16 MHz system clock by the baud rate register (UBRR) to sample each bit at its center — this is why baud rate mismatch causes "garbled" text in the Serial Monitor.

### 3.3 Complete Arduino Sketch Anatomy (Blink + Serial)

```cpp
/* Module Experiment 2: LED + Serial Monitor Interfacing */
int ledPin = 13;               // Built-in LED on most Arduino boards

void setup() {
  pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT);     // Configure pin 13 as OUTPUT
  Serial.begin(9600);          // Initialize UART at 9600 bps
  Serial.println("System Initialized.");
}

void loop() {
  digitalWrite(ledPin, HIGH);  // LED ON → 5V across pin
  Serial.println("LED State: ON");
  delay(1000);                 // Wait 1000 ms

  digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW);   // LED OFF → 0V across pin
  Serial.println("LED State: OFF");
  delay(1000);
}
```

**Program Flow Logic:**
1. `setup()` runs **once** at power-up or reset.
2. `pinMode()` configures the pin direction (INPUT/OUTPUT).
3. `Serial.begin(9600)` initializes UART registers.
4. `loop()` runs **infinitely** at ~16 MHz execution speed, but `delay()` suspends execution for the specified milliseconds.
5. `Serial.println()` transmits a string + carriage return + newline over the TX pin (D1).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Arduino Uno Functional Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PWR["POWER SUBSYSTEM"]
        USB["USB 5V IN"]
        JACK["DC BARREL 7-12V"]
        REG["VOLTAGE REGULATOR 5V"]
    end

    subgraph MCU["ATmega328P MICROCONTROLLER"]
        CPU["CPU 16 MHz"]
        GPIO["GPIO PINS D0-D13"]
        ADC["10-BIT ADC A0-A5"]
        UART["UART HARDWARE"]
        SRAM["2 KB SRAM"]
    end

    subgraph IO["INTERFACE PERIPHERALS"]
        LED["LED + 220 OHM RESISTOR"]
        MON["SERIAL MONITOR PC"]
    end

    USB --> REG
    JACK --> REG
    REG --> CPU
    CPU --> GPIO
    CPU --> UART
    GPIO --> LED
    UART <--> MON
    GPIO --> ADC
```

### 4.2 LED Interfacing Circuit — Current Flow Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["ARDUINO PIN 13 OUTPUT HIGH 5V"] -->|CURRENT FLOWS| B["220 OHM RESISTOR"]
    B -->|VOLTAGE DROPS TO 2V| C["RED LED ANODE"]
    C --> D["LED CATHODE"]
    D -->|CURRENT RETURNS| E["ARDUINO GND 0V"]

    style A fill:#4CAF50,color:#fff
    style E fill:#F44336,color:#fff
    style B fill:#FFC107
    style C fill:#FF9800
```

### 4.3 Serial Monitor Communication Flow (UART Protocol)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PC as PC SERIAL MONITOR
    participant USB as USB-TO-UART BRIDGE
    participant MCU as ATmega328P
    participant LED as LED PIN 13

    Note over PC,USB: INITIALIZATION PHASE
    PC->>USB: Open COM Port @ 9600 bps
    USB->>MCU: DTR Reset Pulse
    MCU->>MCU: Bootloader runs setup()
    MCU->>MCU: Serial.begin 9600
    MCU->>USB: "System Ready\n"
    USB->>PC: Display "System Ready"

    Note over MCU,LED: RUNTIME LOOP
    MCU->>LED: digitalWrite 13 HIGH
    MCU->>USB: "LED ON\n"
    USB->>PC: Display "LED ON"
    PC->>USB: User types "status"
    USB->>MCU: "status\n"
    MCU->>USB: "LED=ON T=25C\n"
    USB->>PC: Display response

    MCU->>LED: digitalWrite 13 LOW
    MCU->>USB: "LED OFF\n"
    USB->>PC: Display "LED OFF"
```

### 4.4 Standard KTU Lab Breadboard Layout (ASCII Schematic)

```
      +5V ──────┐
                │
   PIN 13 ──────┤
                ├──[ 220 Ω ]──┤►|── GND
                │   (R)    (LED)
   PIN 12 ──────┤
                ├──[ 220 Ω ]──┤►|── GND
   PIN 11 ──────┤
                ├──[ 220 Ω ]──┤►|── GND
   PIN 10 ──────┤
                └──[ 220 Ω ]──┤►|── GND

   Legend:
   ┤►|  = LED (Anode → Cathode)
   [R]  = Resistor 220 Ω
   GND  = Common ground rail
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Exam Note**: Always declare `pinMode()` in `setup()`, never inside `loop()`. Setting direction repeatedly wastes CPU cycles and can cause race conditions with external hardware.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define the function `pinMode()` in Arduino. What are the three possible modes and when is each used?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
`pinMode(pin, mode)` is an Arduino function used in `setup()` to configure a digital pin's behavior as either an **INPUT** or **OUTPUT** (or **INPUT_PULLUP**).

- **OUTPUT mode**: Pin is configured to drive external loads like LEDs, buzzers, or relays. It can source or sink up to 40 mA.
- **INPUT mode**: Pin is set to high-impedance state (~100 MΩ) to read external signals from sensors or buttons.
- **INPUT_PULLUP mode**: Same as INPUT, but enables an internal 20–50 kΩ resistor connected to 5V, eliminating the need for an external pull-up resistor.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for modes with examples.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**What is the role of a current limiting resistor in an LED circuit interfaced with an Arduino? Calculate its value for a red LED ($V_f = 2V$, $I = 15\,mA$) connected to a 5V pin.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Role (1 Mark):** An LED is a non-linear device; once forward voltage is exceeded, current rises exponentially and destroys the device. The **current limiting resistor** restricts current to a safe value, protecting the LED from thermal runaway.

**Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$
R = \dfrac{V_s - V_f}{I} = \dfrac{5 - 2}{0.015}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{3}{0.015} = 200\,\Omega
$$

The nearest standard E12 resistor is **220 Ω**, which gives a safe current of ~13.6 mA.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the ATmega328P microcontroller used in Arduino Uno. List its key specifications including flash memory, SRAM, EEPROM, and I/O pin count. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain how to interface **4 LEDs** to Arduino digital pins 10, 11, 12, and 13. Write the complete Arduino program to blink the LEDs in a **sequential pattern (Knight Rider effect)** with a 200 ms delay. Show the expected Serial Monitor output. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The **ATmega328P** is an 8-bit AVR RISC-based CMOS microcontroller by Microchip (formerly Atmel). It forms the brain of the Arduino Uno board.

| Specification | Value |
|---|---|
| Architecture | 8-bit AVR RISC |
| Flash Program Memory | **32 KB** (0.5 KB used by bootloader) |
| SRAM | **2 KB** |
| EEPROM | **1 KB** |
| Digital I/O Pins | **14** (6 with PWM) |
| Analog Input Pins | **6** (10-bit ADC) |
| Clock Speed | **16 MHz** |
| Operating Voltage | **5 V** |
| UART / I2C / SPI | 1 / 1 / 1 |

**Block Diagram Explanation:**
1. **CPU Core**: Harvard architecture with separate program and data buses for single-cycle instruction execution.
2. **Flash Memory**: Stores the user sketch (non-volatile).
3. **SRAM**: Runtime variables, stack, and heap.
4. **EEPROM**: Long-term storage of parameters (e.g., calibration data).
5. **GPIO Ports**: Three ports (B, C, D) mapped to physical pins.
6. **ADC**: Successive approximation type, 10-bit resolution.
7. **Peripherals**: Timer/Counter0, 1, 2; UART; SPI; I2C (TWI); Watchdog Timer.

*(Distribute 7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for specifications table, 2 for peripheral explanation.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Circuit Diagram:**

```
   PIN 10 ──[ 220Ω ]──┤►|── GND
   PIN 11 ──[ 220Ω ]──┤►|── GND
   PIN 12 ──[ 220Ω ]──┤►|── GND
   PIN 13 ──[ 220Ω ]──┤►|── GND
```

**Arduino Program (Knight Rider / Scanner Effect):**

```cpp
int ledPins[] = {10, 11, 12, 13};
int numLeds = 4;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
  Serial.println("Knight Rider Sequence Initiated");
  for (int i = 0; i < numLeds; i++) {
    pinMode(ledPins[i], OUTPUT);
  }
}

void loop() {
  // Forward sweep
  for (int i = 0; i < numLeds; i++) {
    digitalWrite(ledPins[i], HIGH);
    Serial.print("LED ON: Pin ");
    Serial.println(ledPins[i]);
    delay(200);
    digitalWrite(ledPins[i], LOW);
  }
  // Reverse sweep
  for (int i = numLeds - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
    digitalWrite(ledPins[i], HIGH);
    Serial.print("LED ON: Pin ");
    Serial.println(ledPins[i]);
    delay(200);
    digitalWrite(ledPins[i], LOW);
  }
}
```

**Expected Serial Monitor Output:**

```
Knight Rider Sequence Initiated
LED ON: Pin 10
LED ON: Pin 11
LED ON: Pin 12
LED ON: Pin 13
LED ON: Pin 12
LED ON: Pin 11
LED ON: Pin 10
...
```

*(7 marks: 2 for circuit, 3 for code, 1 for Serial output, 1 for logic explanation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **UART communication protocol** used by the Arduino Serial Monitor. Define baud rate, frame format (8N1), and derive the time required to transmit a 25-character string at 9600 bps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Interface a **push button** to Arduino pin 2 and an **LED** to pin 13. Write a program that turns the LED ON when the button is pressed and prints "BUTTON PRESSED" to the Serial Monitor. Use `INPUT_PULLUP` mode. Explain why pull-up resistors are necessary. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**UART Protocol Overview:**
UART is a **full-duplex, asynchronous, serial communication** protocol. It uses two lines: **TX (transmit)** and **RX (receive)**. No shared clock is present; both devices must agree on the **baud rate** in advance.

**Frame Format (8N1):**

| Bit Position | 0 (Idle) | 1 (Start) | 2-9 (Data) | 10 (Stop) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Function | HIGH | LOW (Start) | LSB to MSB | HIGH (Stop) |

- **8** = 8 data bits
- **N** = No parity
- **1** = 1 stop bit
- **Total = 10 bits per frame**

**Derivation for 25-Character String:**

Time per bit:

$$
T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{9600} = 104.17\,\mu s
$$

Time per byte (10 bits):

$$
T_{byte} = 10 \times 104.17\,\mu s = 1.0417\,ms
$$

Total time for 25 characters:

$$
T_{total} = 25 \times 1.0417\,ms = 26.04\,ms
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **theoretical lower bound**. Real-world transmission takes longer due to software processing delays in the Arduino's `loop()` execution.

*(7 marks: 2 for protocol, 2 for frame format, 3 for derivation.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Circuit:**

```
   PIN 2 (INPUT_PULLUP) ──┤ BUTTON ├── GND
   
   PIN 13 ──[ 220Ω ]──┤►|── GND
```

**Arduino Program:**

```cpp
const int buttonPin = 2;
const int ledPin = 13;

void setup() {
  pinMode(buttonPin, INPUT_PULLUP);  // Internal pull-up enabled
  pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT);
  Serial.begin(9600);
  Serial.println("System Ready. Waiting for button press...");
}

void loop() {
  int buttonState = digitalRead(buttonPin);
  
  if (buttonState == LOW) {  // Button pressed (active LOW)
    digitalWrite(ledPin, HIGH);
    Serial.println("BUTTON PRESSED");
    delay(300);  // Software debounce
  } else {
    digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW);
  }
}
```

**Why Pull-up Resistors Are Necessary:**

1. **Floating Pin Problem**: An unconnected input pin acts like an antenna, picking up electromagnetic noise and giving random HIGH/LOW readings.
2. **Defined Logic State**: The pull-up resistor ties the pin to a known 5V (HIGH) state when the button is open.
3. **Active-Low Configuration**: Pressing the button connects the pin to GND (LOW), making the code logic intuitive.
4. **Hardware Simplification**: Eliminates the need for an external 10 kΩ resistor, saving PCB space and cost.

**Debouncing Note**: Mechanical buttons "bounce" (rapidly make-break) for ~5–10 ms. A `delay(300)` in the code suppresses this noise.

*(7 marks: 2 for circuit, 3 for code, 2 for pull-up explanation.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `Serial.begin()`** → Serial Monitor shows nothing. *(Lose 1 mark per occurrence.)*
> 2. **Baud rate mismatch** between `Serial.begin(X)` and the Serial Monitor dropdown. Results in garbled "█▓▒░" characters. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
> 3. **Using `digitalWrite(pin, 1)` instead of `HIGH`** → Works in C++ but considered bad practice. *(Lose 0.5 mark for style.)*
> 4. **Not including a current limiting resistor** for the LED. The LED will burn out in seconds. *(Lose 2 marks for circuit design.)*
> 5. **Connecting LED in reverse polarity** → It will not light up. Always check the anode (longer leg) and cathode (shorter leg, flat edge on the LED body). *(Lose 1 mark.)*
> 6. **Putting `pinMode()` inside `loop()`** → Wasteful CPU usage. Always place it in `setup()`. *(Lose 0.5 mark.)*
> 7. **Forgetting `delay()` after `Serial.println()`** → Text scrolls too fast to read. *(Lose 0.5 mark.)*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2**
>
> - **Arduino UNO** uses the **ATmega328P** 8-bit microcontroller with **32 KB flash, 2 KB SRAM, 16 MHz clock**.
> - **Digital pins** operate at **5V logic**. HIGH = 5V, LOW = 0V.
> - **Maximum pin current** is **40 mA absolute max**, **20 mA recommended continuous**.
> - **Every LED must have a current limiting resistor** calculated as $R = (V_s - V_f) / I_{LED}$.
> - Standard red LED: $V_f \approx 2V$, $I \approx 20\,mA$ → use **220 Ω** resistor.
> - **`pinMode()`** must be called in `setup()` to define INPUT / OUTPUT / INPUT_PULLUP.
> - **`Serial.begin(baud)`** initializes UART. Default Arduino baud rate is **9600 bps**.
> - **8N1 frame format** = 1 Start + 8 Data + 1 Stop = **10 bits/byte**.
> - **Time per bit** = $1 / \text{baud rate}$. At 9600 bps, this is **104.17 μs**.
> - **`INPUT_PULLUP`** enables an internal **20–50 kΩ resistor** to prevent floating pin reads.
> - **`Serial.println()`** sends text + newline; **`Serial.print()`** sends text only.
> - **`delay(ms)`** pauses execution; avoid using it for time-critical control loops.
> - **Active-LOW buttons** (using `INPUT_PULLUP`) read `LOW` when pressed, `HIGH` when released.
> - **LED anode** = longer leg, connects to positive; **cathode** = shorter leg, flat edge, connects to GND.
> - **Baud rate mismatch** between Arduino and Serial Monitor causes garbled output.
> - **Analog pins (A0–A5)** are 10-bit ADC; range 0–1023 corresponding to 0–5V.
> - **PWM pins** (marked ~) are 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11 — used with `analogWrite(pin, 0–255)`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing IR and Ultrasonic Sensor with Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCADL507 - ROBOTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interfacing IR and Ultrasonic Sensor with Arduino

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Infrared (IR) Proximity Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **Infrared (IR) Proximity Sensor** is an electronic device that emits infrared light via an IR LED and detects the reflected IR radiation using a photodiode or phototransistor to determine the presence, absence, or proximity of an object within its detection range without physical contact.

### Pin Configuration (Standard 3-Pin Module)

| Pin Label | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| VCC | +5V DC Power Supply |
| GND | Common Ground (0V Reference) |
| OUT | Digital Output (HIGH/LOW) |

### Working Principle
The IR LED continuously emits **infrared radiation at ~940 nm wavelength**. When an object enters the detection zone, the IR light strikes the object and reflects back toward the photodiode. The onboard comparator (typically **LM393**) compares the reflected light intensity against a threshold set by the on-board potentiometer, producing a clean digital output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:**
> The IR sensor in PCADL507 is configured as a **digital output** module. It returns only two states: `LOW` (no object detected) and `HIGH` (object detected within range). True analog IR distance sensors (Sharp GP2Y0A21) are NOT used in this experiment.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> Imagine standing in a dark room with a torchlight. You shine the torch on the wall — the light bounces back. If someone walks between you and the wall, suddenly less light comes back. The IR sensor works on exactly this principle: **emitted light → bounce → detection**. The threshold is set so that only "enough light bouncing back" (i.e., an object close enough) triggers the alert.

---

## 1.2 Ultrasonic Distance Sensor (HC-SR04)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor** is a non-contact measurement transducer that determines the distance to an object by emitting a **40 kHz ultrasonic pulse** and measuring the time interval between transmission and reception of the echo reflected from the target surface.

### Pin Configuration (4-Pin Module)

| Pin Label | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| VCC | +5V DC Power Supply |
| Trig | Trigger Input (10 µs pulse from Arduino) |
| Echo | Echo Output (HIGH duration = round-trip time) |
| GND | Common Ground (0V Reference) |

### Working Principle
1. Arduino sends a **10 microsecond HIGH pulse** to the Trig pin.
2. The sensor internally emits **8 bursts of 40 kHz ultrasonic sound waves**.
3. The waves travel through air, strike the object, and reflect back.
4. The Echo pin goes HIGH for a duration equal to the round-trip travel time.
5. Arduino measures this duration using `pulseIn()` and computes distance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Range & Limitations (KTU High-Yield Specs):**
> - Effective Range: **2 cm to 400 cm** (≈ 13 feet)
> - Accuracy: **±3 mm**
> - Measurement Angle: **15° cone**
> - Operating Voltage: **+5V DC**, Current: **< 15 mA**

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> Think of a **bat** flying in a cave. It screams (ultrasonic pulse) and listens for the echo. The longer it waits to hear the echo, the farther away the wall is. The HC-SR04 is essentially a **mechanical bat** — it "shouts" sound, "listens" for the bounce, and converts the wait time into distance using the known speed of sound.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Sensors Together? (KTU Exam Hot Topic)

| Aspect | IR Sensor | Ultrasonic Sensor |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Medium** | Light (EM wave) | Sound (Mechanical wave) |
| **Output Type** | Digital (presence only) | Digital timed pulse → Analog distance |
| **Range** | 2–30 cm (short) | 2–400 cm (long) |
| **Surface Dependency** | Color/reflectivity sensitive | Works on most hard surfaces |
| **Response Time** | < 2 ms | ~30 ms cycle |
| **Best Use** | Line-following, obstacle detection flags | Accurate distance measurement |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Ultrasonic Distance Measurement

### Speed of Sound Reference Data

The ultrasonic sensor fundamentally relies on the **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** principle. The distance is derived from the time taken by the sound wave to travel to the object and back.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Constants (Must Memorize):**
> - Speed of sound in air at **20°C**: $v = 343 \text{ m/s} = 34300 \text{ cm/s} = 0.0343 \text{ cm/µs}$
> - The factor of **2** in the formula accounts for the **round-trip** (forward + return).

### Core Governing Equation

Let:
- $t$ = measured echo pulse duration in microseconds (µs)
- $v$ = speed of sound = **0.0343 cm/µs**
- $d$ = one-way distance from sensor to object (cm)

**Round-trip distance** = $v \times t$ (this is the distance traveled by the wave)

**One-way distance** (what we want) = $\dfrac{v \times t}{2}$

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|--------------------|------------|------|-------|
| 1 | One-way distance | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | cm | Round-trip correction |
| 2 | Numerical formula | $d = \dfrac{0.0343 \cdot t}{2} = 0.01715 \cdot t$ | cm | Direct plug-in version |
| 3 | Speed of sound | $v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | m/s | $T$ in °C (temperature-corrected) |
| 4 | Round-trip time | $t = 2d / v$ | µs | Inverse formula |
| 5 | Maximum measurable distance | $d_{max} = \dfrac{v \cdot t_{max}}{2}$ | cm | Sensor limit 400 cm |
| 6 | Minimum measurable distance | $d_{min}$ | cm | Sensor limit 2 cm |
| 7 | IR Detection condition | $V_{reflected} > V_{threshold}$ | V | LM393 comparator trigger |
| 8 | Echo HIGH pulse duration | $t_{echo} = \dfrac{2 \cdot d}{v}$ | µs | Set by Arduino `pulseIn()` |

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The Echo pin of an HC-SR04 connected to Arduino remains HIGH for **588 µs**. Calculate the distance to the object.

**Solution using the formula:**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2} = \frac{0.0343 \text{ cm/µs} \times 588 \text{ µs}}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{20.1684}{2} = 10.0842 \text{ cm} \approx 10 \text{ cm}$$

---

## 2.2 Hardware Connection Theory (Arduino Uno Reference)

### IR Sensor Connection Table

| IR Pin | Arduino Pin | Wire Color (Convention) |
|--------|-------------|--------------------------|
| VCC | **5V** | Red |
| GND | **GND** | Black |
| OUT | Digital Pin **D2** | Yellow |

### Ultrasonic Sensor Connection Table

| HC-SR04 Pin | Arduino Pin | Wire Color (Convention) |
|--------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| VCC | **5V** | Red |
| GND | **GND** | Black |
| Trig | Digital Pin **D9** | Green |
| Echo | Digital Pin **D10** | Blue |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many boards cannot source enough current from a single 5V pin to power both sensors simultaneously with the Ultrasonic sensor's burst transmission. If LED indicators dim or readings are unstable, use an **external 5V supply** with common ground.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Sensor Used | Function |
|--------------------|-------------|----------|
| **Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMR)** | Both | IR for short-range bumper, Ultrasonic for long-range mapping |
| **Automated Parking Systems** | Ultrasonic | Measures distance to walls/parked cars |
| **Reverse Car Alarms** | Ultrasonic | Driver alert for obstacles behind vehicle |
| **Conveyor Belt Object Counters** | IR | Counts items passing through a fixed zone |
| **Robotic Vacuum Cleaners** | Both | Wall-following (IR) + cliff detection (IR) + room mapping (Ultrasonic) |
| **Liquid Level Tanks** | Ultrasonic | Non-contact measurement from tank top |
| **Security Systems** | IR | Motion/intrusion detection beams |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ultrasonic Distance Equation

### Starting Point: Kinematic Equation for Constant Velocity

For sound traveling at a constant velocity $v$ in still air:

$$\text{Distance} = \text{Velocity} \times \text{Time}$$

### Step 1: Define the round-trip geometry

The ultrasonic pulse travels from the sensor to the object and back. If the one-way distance is $d$, the total distance traveled by the sound wave is:

$$D_{total} = 2d$$

### Step 2: Apply the kinematic equation

$$D_{total} = v \times t$$

$$2d = v \times t$$

### Step 3: Solve for $d$ (one-way distance)

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$$

### Step 4: Substitute standard speed of sound

The speed of sound in dry air at **20°C** is **343 m/s**. Converting to cm/µs:

$$v = 343 \text{ m/s} \times \frac{100 \text{ cm}}{1 \text{ m}} \times \frac{1 \text{ s}}{10^6 \text{ µs}} = 0.0343 \text{ cm/µs}$$

### Step 5: Final compact formula used in Arduino code

$$d = \frac{0.0343 \times t}{2} = 0.01715 \times t \;\; (\text{in cm})$$

### Step 6: Temperature correction (advanced KTU follow-up)

The speed of sound varies with air temperature $T$ (in °C):

$$v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \;\; \text{(m/s)}$$

For example, at $T = 25°C$:

$$v = 331 + 0.6 \times 25 = 331 + 15 = 346 \text{ m/s} = 0.0346 \text{ cm/µs}$$

This corrected value would yield a slightly different distance. For KTU lab work, the constant **0.0343 cm/µs** is sufficient unless explicitly stated otherwise.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Arduino Program Flow for Combined Sensor Interface

### Complete Annotated Arduino Sketch

```cpp
// =====================================================
// PCADL507 - Robotics Lab
// Module Experiment 2: IR + Ultrasonic with Arduino
// =====================================================

// --- Pin Definitions ---
const int IR_PIN       = 2;     // Digital pin for IR OUT
const int TRIG_PIN     = 9;     // HC-SR04 Trig
const int ECHO_PIN     = 10;    // HC-SR04 Echo

// --- Constants ---
const float SPEED_OF_SOUND = 0.0343;  // cm/microsecond
const float DISTANCE_FACTOR = SPEED_OF_SOUND / 2.0; // 0.01715

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);          // Initialize Serial Monitor at 9600 baud
  pinMode(IR_PIN,   INPUT);    // IR OUT pin is an INPUT to Arduino
  pinMode(TRIG_PIN, OUTPUT);   // Trig pin is OUTPUT from Arduino
  pinMode(ECHO_PIN, INPUT);    // Echo pin is INPUT to Arduino
}

void loop() {
  // ---- Step 1: IR Sensor Read (digital) ----
  int irState = digitalRead(IR_PIN);

  // ---- Step 2: Trigger Ultrasonic ----
  digitalWrite(TRIG_PIN, LOW);
  delayMicroseconds(2);                 // Settle line
  digitalWrite(TRIG_PIN, HIGH);
  delayMicroseconds(10);                // 10 µs trigger pulse
  digitalWrite(TRIG_PIN, LOW);

  // ---- Step 3: Read Echo Pulse Duration ----
  long duration = pulseIn(ECHO_PIN, HIGH, 30000); // timeout 30 ms

  // ---- Step 4: Convert to Distance ----
  float distance_cm = duration * DISTANCE_FACTOR;

  // ---- Step 5: Output to Serial Monitor ----
  Serial.print("IR: ");
  Serial.print(irState == HIGH ? "OBJECT" : "CLEAR");
  Serial.print("  |  Distance: ");
  Serial.print(distance_cm);
  Serial.println(" cm");

  delay(200);                          // Sampling rate ~5 Hz
}
```

### Expected Serial Monitor Output

```
IR: CLEAR  |  Distance: 25.42 cm
IR: OBJECT |  Distance: 8.91 cm
IR: OBJECT |  Distance: 4.73 cm
IR: CLEAR  |  Distance: 15.07 cm
```

### Function-by-Function Analytical Matrix

| Function Call | Internal Working | KTU Key Concept |
|---------------|------------------|-----------------|
| `pinMode(pin, INPUT)` | Configures pin as high-impedance input | IR OUT, Echo pins act as INPUT |
| `digitalWrite(TRIG, HIGH)` | Outputs 5V on D9 | Initiates ultrasonic burst |
| `delayMicroseconds(10)` | Halts CPU for 10 µs | Minimum trigger width per datasheet |
| `pulseIn(ECHO, HIGH)` | Counts microseconds while Echo stays HIGH | Returns round-trip time |
| `Serial.print()` | UART transmission at 9600 baud | Used for observation in lab |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always mention `pulseIn()` returns the duration in **microseconds** and waits up to a **timeout** (default 1 second). If no echo is received, it returns **0**. Add a sanity check before dividing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hardware Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    psuA[Arduino 5V Rail] --> irA[IR Sensor VCC]
    psuA[Arduino 5V Rail] --> usA[Ultrasonic VCC]
    gndA[Arduino GND] --> irB[IR Sensor GND]
    gndA[Arduino GND] --> usB[Ultrasonic GND]
    d2A[Arduino D2 INPUT] --> irC[IR OUT Signal]
    d9A[Arduino D9 OUTPUT] --> usC[Trig Pulse 10us]
    d10A[Arduino D10 INPUT] --> usD[Echo Pulse Width]
    irC --> objA[Object Detection Logic]
    usC --> trigA[Trigger Burst Generator]
    usD --> echoA[Time of Flight Capture]
    echoA --> calcA[Distance Calculator 0.01715]
    objA --> monA[Serial Monitor Display]
    calcA --> monA[Serial Monitor Display]
```

## 4.2 Program Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start loop] --> readIR[Read IR Pin D2]
    readIR --> trigLow[Set TRIG LOW 2us]
    trigLow --> trigHigh[Set TRIG HIGH 10us]
    trigHigh --> trigLow2[Set TRIG LOW]
    trigLow2 --> pulseWait[pulseIn ECHO HIGH]
    pulseWait --> checkTime{Timeout or valid?}
    checkTime -->|Timeout| errorH[Display Out of Range]
    checkTime -->|Valid| computeD[distance = time x 0.01715]
    computeD --> printOut[Print IR state and Distance]
    errorH --> printOut
    printOut --> delayH[Delay 200 ms]
    delayH --> startA
```

## 4.3 Sensor Working Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IR_Block[IR Proximity Sensor Path]
        irLED[IR LED Emits 940nm Light] --> irProp[Light Travels to Object]
        irProp --> irReflect[Light Reflects Back]
        irReflect --> irPhoto[Photodiode Detects]
        irPhoto --> irComp[LM393 Comparator]
        irComp --> irOut[Digital HIGH or LOW]
    end

    subgraph US_Block[Ultrasonic Sensor Path]
        usTrig[Arduino 10us Trigger] --> usBurst[Sensor Emits 8 Pulses 40kHz]
        usBurst --> usTravel[Sound Travels to Object]
        usTravel --> usEcho[Sound Returns]
        usEcho --> usEchoPin[Echo Pin HIGH for duration]
        usEchoPin --> usCalc[Arduino Computes Distance]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Note:** Physical pin-level circuit diagrams using schematic symbols (LEDs, resistors, ICs) are typically drawn on paper during the lab exam. The block diagrams above show the **functional architecture** and signal flow, which is what KTU examiners reward in viva and record submissions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Explain the working principle of an IR proximity sensor. Mention any two applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An IR proximity sensor consists of an IR LED (emitter) and a photodiode (receiver). The IR LED emits infrared light at **940 nm wavelength**. When an object enters the detection range, the IR light reflects off the object and strikes the photodiode. The change in light intensity is converted to a voltage change, which is compared against a threshold by an on-board LM393 comparator, producing a clean digital output (HIGH when object detected, LOW otherwise).

**Applications (any two):**
1. Line-following robots (detecting black line on white surface).
2. Obstacle detection in autonomous vehicles.
3. Object counters on conveyor belts.
4. Touchless soap/water dispensers.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**State the speed of sound in air and derive the distance formula used by the HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The speed of sound in air at **20°C** is **343 m/s** = **0.0343 cm/µs**.

Derivation:

$$d = \frac{v \times t}{2} = \frac{0.0343 \times t}{2} = 0.01715 \times t \;\; (\text{cm})$$

where $d$ is the one-way distance in cm and $t$ is the round-trip echo pulse duration in microseconds measured by `pulseIn()`.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks**. Candidates answer **ONE** of two alternatives. Each sub-part tests **Apply / Analyze** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of an HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor with Arduino Uno. Write the Arduino code to display the measured distance on the Serial Monitor.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram).**

**Connections:**
- VCC → 5V
- GND → GND
- Trig → D9
- Echo → D10

**Arduino Code:**

```cpp
const int trigPin = 9;
const int echoPin = 10;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
  pinMode(trigPin, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(echoPin, INPUT);
}

void loop() {
  digitalWrite(trigPin, LOW);
  delayMicroseconds(2);
  digitalWrite(trigPin, HIGH);
  delayMicroseconds(10);
  digitalWrite(trigPin, LOW);

  long duration = pulseIn(echoPin, HIGH);
  float distance = duration * 0.0343 / 2.0;

  Serial.print("Distance: ");
  Serial.print(distance);
  Serial.println(" cm");

  delay(200);
}
```

**Working:** A 10 µs trigger pulse initiates 8 bursts of 40 kHz sound. The echo pin stays HIGH for the round-trip duration, measured by `pulseIn()`. Distance is computed using the formula $d = 0.01715 \times t$. (Full 7 marks)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**An HC-SR04 sensor returns an echo pulse of duration 1176 µs. Calculate the distance to the object. If the temperature of the lab is 40°C, what would be the corrected distance?**

**Model Solution:**

**Standard case (20°C, $v = 0.0343$ cm/µs):**

$$d = \frac{0.0343 \times 1176}{2} = \frac{40.3368}{2} = 20.17 \text{ cm}$$

**Corrected case (T = 40°C):**

$$v = 331 + 0.6 \times 40 = 355 \text{ m/s} = 0.0355 \text{ cm/µs}$$

$$d_{corrected} = \frac{0.0355 \times 1176}{2} = \frac{41.748}{2} = 20.87 \text{ cm}$$

**Observation:** The corrected distance is higher because sound travels faster at higher temperatures. (Full 7 marks)

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Interface an IR proximity sensor with Arduino Uno such that an LED glows when an object is detected within range and remains OFF otherwise. Draw the connection diagram and write the program.**

**Model Solution:**

**Connections:**
- IR VCC → 5V
- IR GND → GND
- IR OUT → D2
- LED Anode → D13 (via 220 Ω resistor)
- LED Cathode → GND

**Arduino Code:**

```cpp
const int irPin = 2;
const int ledPin = 13;

void setup() {
  pinMode(irPin, INPUT);
  pinMode(ledPin, OUTPUT);
  Serial.begin(9600);
}

void loop() {
  int state = digitalRead(irPin);
  if (state == HIGH) {
    digitalWrite(ledPin, HIGH);
    Serial.println("Object Detected");
  } else {
    digitalWrite(ledPin, LOW);
    Serial.println("No Object");
  }
  delay(100);
}
```

**Working:** The IR sensor output goes HIGH when an object reflects IR light back to the photodiode. The Arduino reads this and drives the LED accordingly. (Full 7 marks)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare IR and Ultrasonic sensors based on (i) principle of operation (ii) range (iii) output type (iv) surface dependency (v) response time (vi) two application areas each. Justify which is more suitable for a long-range obstacle-avoiding autonomous robot.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | IR Sensor | Ultrasonic Sensor |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| Principle | Reflected IR light | Reflected sound waves |
| Range | 2–30 cm | 2–400 cm |
| Output | Digital (HIGH/LOW) | Digital timed pulse → distance |
| Surface | Color/reflectivity dependent | Works on most hard surfaces |
| Response | < 2 ms | ~30 ms cycle |

**Applications:**
- IR: line-following, edge detection, object counters.
- Ultrasonic: distance mapping, parking sensors, liquid level.

**Justification:** For **long-range obstacle avoidance**, the **Ultrasonic sensor is more suitable** because it provides actual distance values (not just presence), covers up to **400 cm**, and is independent of surface color. The IR sensor only gives a binary output within **30 cm**, making it unsuitable for long-range detection. (Full 7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgot the factor of 2:** The most common mistake is computing $d = v \times t$ instead of $d = (v \times t)/2$. Loss: 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Wrong units:** Mixing m/s and µs without converting. Always use the shortcut $d = 0.01715 \times t$ in cm.
> 3. **Pin direction confusion:** Many students mark Trig as INPUT and Echo as OUTPUT. Remember: Trig = OUTPUT from Arduino, Echo = INPUT to Arduino.
> 4. **Missing timeout:** `pulseIn()` blocks the program indefinitely if no echo returns. Always specify a timeout value (e.g., 30000 µs) to avoid hangs.
> 5. **No trigger pulse stabilization:** Skipping `delayMicroseconds(2)` before the 10 µs HIGH pulse can cause inconsistent triggering.
> 6. **Confusing analog and digital IR:** If the question specifies a Sharp analog IR sensor, the answer changes entirely — for KTU PCADL507, the **digital 3-pin IR module** is the standard.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [x] **IR sensor** uses **940 nm infrared light** and the **LM393 comparator**.
- [x] IR sensor has **3 pins**: VCC, GND, OUT (digital output).
- [x] **HC-SR04** uses **40 kHz ultrasonic bursts** (8 cycles per trigger).
- [x] HC-SR04 has **4 pins**: VCC, Trig, Echo, GND.
- [x] Trigger pulse must be **exactly 10 µs** HIGH.
- [x] **Speed of sound** at 20°C = **343 m/s = 0.0343 cm/µs**.
- [x] **Distance formula**: $d = \dfrac{0.0343 \times t}{2} = 0.01715 \times t$ (in cm).
- [x] Round-trip **factor of 2** is mandatory — sound goes and returns.
- [x] `pulseIn(echoPin, HIGH)` returns duration in **microseconds**.
- [x] HC-SR04 range: **2 cm to 400 cm**, accuracy **±3 mm**.
- [x] Temperature correction: $v = 331 + 0.6 \times T$ (T in °C).
- [x] For long-range applications → **Ultrasonic**; for short-range presence → **IR**.
- [x] Add **timeout** to `pulseIn()` to prevent program hang.
- [x] Use **delay(200)** between readings for stable sampling.
- [x] For lab exam: also know the **role of potentiometer** on IR sensor (sets detection threshold).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing DC Motors with Arduino - Speed and Direction Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCADL507 - ROBOTICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Interfacing DC Motors with Arduino - Speed and Direction Control ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **DC Motor Interfacing** refers to the electronic process of connecting a Direct Current (DC) motor to a microcontroller unit (Arduino) through an intermediate **motor driver IC** (typically the **L293D** or **L298N** H-Bridge) so that the microcontroller can command the motor's **rotational speed** (via Pulse Width Modulation, PWM) and **rotational direction** (via polarity reversal of the applied voltage). In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Robotics Lab (PCADL507)**, this experiment is classified under **Actuator Interfacing** and is considered a foundational prerequisite for mobile robotics, wheeled autonomous vehicles, and robotic manipulator design. The motor is treated as a **non-linear electromechanical transducer** that converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation. ### 1.2 Intuition & Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — The Train Track Switcher** > Imagine a train running on two parallel rails: > - **Forward Direction**: Current flows from Rail A → Rail B (Left to Right). > - **Reverse Direction**: Current flows from Rail B → Rail A (Right to Left). > - **Speed Control**: A dimmer switch that flickers the power ON/OFF rapidly. The longer it stays ON, the faster the train runs. This flickering is **PWM**. > - **The Microcontroller (Arduino)** is the **signal tower operator** deciding which rail to energise and how fast. > - **The Motor Driver (H-Bridge)** is the **heavy-duty track switcher**, because the Arduino itself cannot supply the high current the train (motor) needs. > [!NOTE] > **Why we cannot connect a DC Motor directly to an Arduino pin:** > An Arduino digital pin can source a maximum of **~40 mA**, while a standard hobby DC motor (e.g., BO, TT, Johnson motor) typically draws **200 mA – 2 A**. Connecting it directly would burn the ATmega328P microcontroller. Hence, an **H-Bridge driver IC** is mandatory. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Hardware Specifications | Parameter | Arduino UNO | Standard DC Motor | L293D Driver | L298N Driver | |---|---|---|---|---| | Logic Voltage | **5 V DC** | 3 V – 12 V DC | **5 V** (VCC1) | **5 V** | | Motor Voltage | N/A | **6 V – 12 V** | **Up to 36 V** | **Up to 46 V** | | Continuous Current | **40 mA / pin** | 200 mA – 2 A | **600 mA / channel** | **2 A / channel** | | PWM Frequency | **~490 Hz / 980 Hz** | N/A | Logic-level | Logic-level | | Pins Used | D5, D6, D9, D10, D11 | 2 terminals | 6 pins | 6 pins | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight**: The default PWM frequency on Arduino pins **D5** and **D6** is **~980 Hz**, while pins **D9, D10, D11** operate at **~490 Hz**. This is non-configurable in standard `analogWrite()` calls using the default Timer/Counter prescaler. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — The H-Bridge Topology The **H-Bridge** is so named because the circuit diagram visually resembles the capital letter **"H"**, with the DC motor placed as the central crossbar and four switching transistors (S1, S2, S3, S4) forming the four vertical legs. **Four Operational States of an H-Bridge:** | State | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | Motor Behaviour | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Forward Rotation** | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | Current flows V+ → Motor → GND | | **Reverse Rotation** | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | Current flows GND → Motor → V+ | | **Brake / Stop (Low)** | OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | Motor coasts, then halts | | **Brake / Stop (Fast)** | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | Motor terminals shorted, dynamic brake | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL SAFETY RULE — Shoot-Through** > Never activate S1 & S2 simultaneously (or S3 & S4) on the same leg. This creates a direct short circuit across the power supply (V+ to GND), causing immediate driver IC failure due to **shoot-through current**. The L293D has internal flyback diodes to protect against inductive kickback but NOT against shoot-through. ### 2.2 Direction Control Logic The direction of a brushed DC motor is governed by the **polarity of the voltage applied across its terminals**: $$V_{motor} = V_A - V_B$$ If $V_A > V_B$, current flows from terminal A to B → **Clockwise (CW) rotation** (by convention). If $V_A < V_B$, current flows from terminal B to A → **Counter-Clockwise (CCW) rotation**. For the **L293D**, this translates to two logic inputs per channel: | Input Pin (e.g., IN1) | Input Pin (e.g., IN2) | Motor Terminal A (OUT1) | Motor Terminal B (OUT2) | Motion | |---|---|---|---|---| | HIGH | LOW | HIGH | LOW | Forward | | LOW | HIGH | LOW | HIGH | Reverse | | HIGH | HIGH | LOW | LOW | Fast Stop (Brake) | | LOW | LOW | HIGH (High-Z) | HIGH (High-Z) | Coast / Stop | ### 2.3 Speed Control via Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) > [!NOTE] > **PWM Definition**: Pulse Width Modulation is a technique where a digital signal is switched ON and OFF at a fixed frequency, and the *ratio* of ON-time to the total period is varied to deliver a controllable **average voltage** to a load. The Arduino's `analogWrite(pin, value)` function generates PWM, where `value` ranges from **0 (0% duty cycle)** to **255 (100% duty cycle)**. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | Formula / Equation | Symbol Description | Units / Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---| | $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}}$ | Duty Cycle ($D$), ON-Time ($T_{ON}$), OFF-Time ($T_{OFF}$) | $0 \le D \le 1$ (or 0% – 100%) | | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ | Average DC voltage seen by motor | Volts (V) | | $analogWrite(pin, x) \Rightarrow D = \dfrac{x}{255}$ | Maps 8-bit PWM value to duty cycle | $0 \le x \le 255$ | | $V_{no-load} = \omega_0 \cdot K_e$ | Back-EMF constant ($K_e$) relates speed to voltage | rad/s, V | | $T_{motor} = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Torque constant ($K_t$) relates current to torque | N·m, A | | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$ | Mechanical power output | Watts | | $I_{stall} = \dfrac{V}{R_a}$ | Stall current at locked rotor (worst case) | A | | $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \cdot 100\%$ | Electrical-to-mechanical efficiency | Percentage (%) | | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot (TOP + 1)}$ | Timer frequency formula (Arduino) | Hz; $f_{clk} = 16$ MHz | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall — Confusing `analogWrite()` with True Analog Output!** > The Arduino has **no true Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** on standard UNO models (except the MKR series and Zero). `analogWrite()` produces a **PWM square wave**, not a smooth analog voltage. For DC motors, this is acceptable because their mechanical inertia filters the pulses into smooth rotation. For audio or precision instrumentation, a DAC is required. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial Robotics**: Conveyor belts, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), CNC feed drives. - **Consumer Electronics**: Cooling fans, camera gimbals, robotic vacuum cleaners (e.g., Roomba). - **Automotive**: Power windows, seat adjusters, electric vehicle traction motors. - **Medical**: Infusion pumps, surgical robot actuators, prosthetics. - **Aerospace**: Drone propeller control (with ESC instead of H-Bridge for brushless motors). ## 3.
